coredata.dc:title: In situ green synthesis of Rosa sterilis-based carbon dots for Fe3+ fluorescent sensing in baijiu
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hongqin
Huang, Guohuan
Li, Yuanlin
Huang, Yongguang
coredata.dc:description: Baijiu is susceptible to contamination by Fe3+ during brewing and storage, and it is crucial to select a convenient method for the detection of Fe3+. In this work, we developed a one-step hydrothermal in situ green synthesis of blue fluorescent carbon dots (RS-CDs) from Rosa sterilis. A series of characterization of RS-CDs was carried out, and it was found that RS-CDs derived from biomass materials were environmentally friendly and low toxic, with an average particle size of 2.75 nm, a graphitic structure, and a surface rich in functional groups related to C, N, and O atoms. In addition, the PL of RS-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent behavior. The emission center was redshifted from 448 nm to 530 nm at excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 460 nm. After investigating the selectivity of RS-CDs for different metal ions, we found that RS-CDs had the highest quenching efficiency for Fe3+ and exhibited a static quenching mechanism. The LOD was 0.959 μM in the linear range of 0–140 μM. In addition, RS-CDs were successfully used for the detection of Fe3+ in four kinds of basic flavor baijiu, as well as in different containers for storage of baijiu. The study demonstrated that Rosa sterilis can be used to produce green and low-cost CDs, and the prepared RS-CDs can be conveniently, rapidly and selectively detected Fe3+ in the baijiu system, which is of potential application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rosa sterilis
Nano-materials
Metal ions
Fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanistic analysis of Fe3+ detection by RS-CDs
Detection of real baijiu samples by RS-CDs
Analysis of Fe3+ in baijiu with different aroma by standard addition method
Detection of RS-CDs on baijiu samples stored in different containers
Mechanism of Fe3+ detection by RS-CDs
Application of RS-CDs for Fe3+ sensing
Stress detection of Fe3+ in baijiu samples stored in different containers by RS-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The support of the current project was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General horizontal project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8), Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327), and Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK[2024] Major Project 016).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: In situ green synthesis of Rosa sterilis-based CDs for Fe3+ sensing in baijiu. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Synthetic route of RS-CDs. (b) TEM of RS-CDs (inset: HRTEM image). (c) Particle size distribution. (d) XRD of RS-CDs. (e) FT-IR spectra of RS-CDs.
(a) XPS gross spectrum of RS-CDs (inset shows elemental content). (b) C1s spectra of RS-CDs. (c) O1s spectra of RS-CDs. (d) N1s spectra of RS-CDs.

(a) UV absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RS-CDs. (b) CIE (1931) spectrogram of RS-CDs. (c) Emission of RS-CDs at varying excitation wavelengths. (d) Emission of RS-CDs at varying concentrations.

(a) RS-CDs fluorescence emission intensity ratio F/F0 for different metal ions at a concentration of 2400 μM. (b) RS-CDs fluorescence emission profiles after different metal ions at a concentration of 2400 μM (inset (left) is a fluorescence photo under UV when Fe3+ was not added, and inset (right) is a fluorescence photo under UV when Fe3+ was supplemented at 1 mM). (c) Quenching effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence of CDs with other metal ions. (d) Sensitivity and stability of RS-CDs for the detection of Fe3+.

(a) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of RS-CDs. (b) UV absorption mechanisms of RS-CDs, Fe3+ and mixtures of RS-CDs and Fe3+. (c) Schematic diagram of the fluorescence quench mechanism of RS-CDs by Fe3+.
Spiked recoveries of Fe3+ in different aroma baijiu by RS-CDs. (a) Sauce-flavor baijiu. (b) Strong-flavor baijiu. (c) Light-flavor baijiu. (d) Rice-flavor baijiu.
Measurement of Fe3+ content in different containers (old enamel tanks, stainless steel tanks, earthenware jars) for baijiu storage.

head.ce:title: In situ green synthesis of Rosa sterilis-based carbon dots for Fe3+ fluorescent sensing in baijiu

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Digital Baijiu Industry Development Research Institute Co., Ltd, Guiyang, Guizhou, 550081, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guizhou Digital Baijiu Industry Development Research Institute Co.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou Digital Baijiu Industry Development Research Institute Co., Ltd. Guiyang, Guizhou 550081, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Baijiu is susceptible to contamination by Fe3+ during brewing and storage, and it is crucial to select a convenient method for the detection of Fe3+. In this work, we developed a one-step hydrothermal in situ green synthesis of blue fluorescent carbon dots (RS-CDs) from Rosa sterilis. A series of characterization of RS-CDs was carried out, and it was found that RS-CDs derived from biomass materials were environmentally friendly and low toxic, with an average particle size of 2.75 nm, a graphitic structure, and a surface rich in functional groups related to C, N, and O atoms. In addition, the PL of RS-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent behavior. The emission center was redshifted from 448 nm to 530 nm at excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 460 nm. After investigating the selectivity of RS-CDs for different metal ions, we found that RS-CDs had the highest quenching efficiency for Fe3+ and exhibited a static quenching mechanism. The LOD was 0.959 μM in the linear range of 0–140 μM. In addition, RS-CDs were successfully used for the detection of Fe3+ in four kinds of basic flavor baijiu, as well as in different containers for storage of baijiu. The study demonstrated that Rosa sterilis can be used to produce green and low-cost CDs, and the prepared RS-CDs can be conveniently, rapidly and selectively detected Fe3+ in the baijiu system, which is of potential application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Baijiu, an alcoholic drink originating in China, distilled from grain or rice, is a traditional fermented food in China that has a wide range of industrial applications. It has four basic flavor types (sauce, strong, clear, and rice) and eight other flavor types. However, because of the use of raw materials, utensils, production, storage and transportation, baijiu inevitably comes into contact with metals during brewing and storage. Some iron utensils, such as iron boilers, baijiu fermentation tanks, baijiu equipment and baijiu storage containers, are prone to release metal ions and cause fluctuations in the Fe3+ content in baijiu, thus affecting its taste and flavor (Moutsatsou et al., 2003; Turner, 2019). As a result, it plays an essential role in developing an easy, low-cost and sensitive method to realize the rapid detection of Fe3+ during the brewing process of baijiu, which will be particularly significant for the quality control and traceability of Fe3+ during baijiu brewing process.

Conventional methods for detecting Fe3+ include electrochemical methods (Brunner et al., 2022), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Michalke, 2022) and atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Costa et al., 2023). However, there were inevitably disadvantages such as high cost, cumbersome pretreatment processes, and harsh conditions, which greatly restrict their applicability. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of nanoparticles, which are usually used in chemical analysis, biosensing and bioimaging due to their excellent optical properties, favorable biocompatibility, water solubility, low toxicity, as well as stability (Cao et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2024). Ma et al. (Ma et al., 2023) used wood persimmon as the carbon source to prepare CDs, which were then used as Fe3+ sensors in water. Zhang et al. (Zhang et al., 2023) doped boric acid into Chinese medicine residue to prepare Fe3+-sensitive fluorescent sensors. These studies demonstrated that the fluorescence change of CDs revealed a fine linear association with the concentration of Fe3+ within a certain range, thus enabling the rapid, sensitive and convenient quantitative detection of Fe3+ (Liu et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022a; Zhao et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2023). Unfortunately, most CDs for Fe3+ detection were limited to aqueous environments and were rarely applied in solid-state fermentation product media, especially in baijiu systems, which may be due to the complexity of the baijiu solid-state fermentation matrices. In this paper, we further investigated the applicability of CDs as fluorescent probes for detecting Fe3+ in baijiu systems, building upon previous studies. Our research highlighted that by utilizing a functional precursor to synthesize CDs, which exhibited unique optical properties and surface chemical activities, these probes can engage in complexation reactions with Fe3+ in baijiu. Therefore, this interaction led to changes in the fluorescence of CDs, allowing us to establish a quantitative relationship between the fluorescence changes of CDs and the concentration of Fe3+. Obviously, relative to the traditional analytical methods, the CDs-based fluorescent probe has more obvious advantages in the detection of harmful substances in baijiu, and it is a popular detection method with great potential.

Rosa sterilis, also known as seedless Rosa roxbunghii, is a wild resource unique to Anshun City, Guizhou Province, containing active substances such as vitamin C, superoxide dismutase, flavonoids and polyphenols. The preparation of carbon dots from Rosa sterilis is expected to preserve the beneficial properties of these compounds within the structure of the carbon dots, thereby imparting a diverse array of surface functional groups. However, the application of Rosa sterilis as a precursor and developed as an assay probe as well as a sensor has rarely been reported. Therefore, in this study, Rosa sterilis was used as a carbon-based material to prepare Rosa sterilis-based CDs (RS-CDs) using an in-situ hydrothermal method, and the response of RS-CDs to metal ions in baijiu was studied. Additionally, the release of metal ions from baijiu stored in different containers was determined using RS-CDs. Our study showed that the active components in Rosa sterilis fruits can be effectively converted into carbon dot functional groups, particularly hydroxyl groups. Consequently, RS-CDs exhibited significant potential for applications in metal ion sensing. Furthermore, RS-CDs derived from biomass possessed excellent fluorescence properties, water solubility, and biocompatibility. The effective strategy proposed in this study for detecting Fe3+ in baijiu, utilizing nanomaterials derived from biomass, serves as a theoretical foundation for achieving rational storage and quality monitoring of baijiu (Fig. 1 ).

The experimental water was deionized and purified using a Millipore system. Analytically pure sodium chloride, calcium chloride, potassium chloride, ferric chloride, copper sulfate, zinc sulfate, aluminum chloride, and lead chloride were offered by Adamas Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while manganese sulfate, chromium chloride, cadmium chloride, cobalt nitrate, and silver nitrate were obtained from Sinotrade Chemical Reagent Co. The purity of all the standards was ≥98%.
A total of 4 flavor types were purchased from the local market. The list of samples with volume, concentration, and place they were sourced is shown in Table S1.

RS-CDs were synthesized in situ by a muffle furnace (SX2-4-10N). Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by a UV-8000ST spectrophotometer. Morphology and size of RS-CDs were characterized by a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out on a Nicolet iS 10 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was determined by an EscaLab 250Xi spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were acquired by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (BrukerAXS, Germany). The fluorescence lifetimes of RS-CDs were determined by FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The sensing of Fe3+ by RS-CDs were determined by Agilent 5110 (ICP-OES) (Agilent Technologies, USA). Cell viability tests were performed on a multifunctional enzyme marker (SpectraMax M5).

Firstly, we juiced the Rosa sterilis fruits and then added 25 mL of juice to a PTFE-lined autoclave and sonicated for 15 min until homogeneous dissolution. The solution was shifted to a muffle furnace, heated to 180 °C for 8 h, and subsequently permitted to return to ambient temperature. The brown solution underwent centrifugation at 10000 r/min for 10 min, followed by further purification using a filter membrane of 0.22 μm. To acquire pure RS-CDs, the purified solution was dialyzed for 24 h in a 1000 Da dialysis bag. Additionally, the resulting brown powder was vacuum freeze-dried and preserved at 4 °C for further characterization and application.

To examine the selection of Fe3+ by RS-CDs in baijiu, different metal ions, namely K+, Ca2+, Na+, Ag+, Al3+, Cd2+, Co+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+, were added to RS-CDs solution using ethanol-water solution with 53% volume fraction of ethanol formulated with a final ion concentration of 800 μM. The selectivity of the RS-CDs solution for 13 metal ions in the simulated baijiu system was investigated. In order to identify the limit of detection (LOD) of RS-CDs for detecting Fe3+, a series of solutions with varying Fe3+ concentrations (final concentrations of 0–1 mM) were prepared at 25 °C to investigate the quantification pattern of RS-CDs for Fe3+ in the simulated baijiu system. Based on the same conditions, Fe3+ was mixed with other metal ions to explore the interference of RS-CDs in the detection of Fe3+ in the simulated baijiu system. For the sensing of metal ions, 3600 μL of 53% anhydrous ethanol was mixed with 400 μL of RS-CDs (1.5 mg/mL) and 2000 μL of different metal ions, and the fluorescence spectra of each group of samples were measured following incubation at room temperature for 10 min (λex = 355 nm, λem = 453 nm), and the blank samples were set as F0. Three times were repeated for all experiments.

To achieve the best possible conditions for detecting Fe3+, different experimental parameters were optimized, including UV bleaching resistance, pH, and incubation time. First, an aqueous solution of 100 μg/mL of RS-CDs was prepared and irradiated with a UV irradiation lamp at 365 nm, and its PL intensity was measured every 30 min. Then, 200 μL of RS-CDs solution (concentration 1.5 mg/mL) and 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution of various pH values (2-12) were sequentially added into a quartz cuvette, shaken and equilibrated for 1 min, and subsequently positioned in a fluorescence spectrophotometer to measure the fluorescence emission spectra with 355 nm as the excitation wavelength. Finally, the optimal reaction time of RS-CDs from 0 to 10 min and the fluorescence stability of RS-CDs from 0 to 60 min in the presence of Fe3+ were determined. To investigate their effects on the fluorescence quenching efficiency, the fluorescence intensity F/F0 was plotted against different influencing factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic analysis of Fe3+ detection by RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: First, 1 mL of 0.01 and 0.1 mg/mL RS-CDs solutions were added to 1 mL of 100 μM Fe3+, which was fixed to 5 mL of water, and the fluorescence decay curves were scanned with an Edinburgh FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer; the excitation wavelength for the test was set to be 375 nm. Subsequently, the RS-CDs, Fe3+, and the mixture of RS-CDs and Fe3+ were separately probed to determine the UV spectral changes (200–800 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of real baijiu samples by RS-CDs
Analysis of Fe3+ in baijiu with different aroma by standard addition method
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practical application of the fluorescent probe, different typical baijiu samples were purchased from the market, a certain concentration of Fe3+ solution was added, and the Fe3+ content was detected by RS-CDs to evaluate recovery. Samples without the Fe3+ solution were used as blank controls. Then, all the experiments were repeated at least thrice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of RS-CDs on baijiu samples stored in different containers
ce:section.ce:para: A pottery altar, a stainless steel tank, and an old enamel cylinder were used as containers to store clear-flavored baijiu HX for 30 d. Subsequently, Fe3+ was detected in the stored baijiu samples by RS-CDs and the results were compared with those of an inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry analyzer (ICP-OES) to evaluate the accuracy and feasibility of the RS-CDs in detecting Fe3+ in different flavored baijiu. In this study, all the involved experiments were performed at least three times.

To determine biocompatibility, cell viability was investigated by CCK-8 kit, thus examining the cytotoxicity of different levels of RS-CDs. Human colon epithelial cells (SO146698) were accessed in 96-well plates and cultured overnight in a 37 °C thermostat containing 5% CO₂. The cells were then treated with RS-CDs at different concentrations (0–0.3 mg/mL) and further incubated for 24 h. Then, medium containing 10% CCK-8 was added in the amount of 200 μL per well, which was placed in a constant temperature incubator to continue incubation for 2 h. Finally, the cell viability at 450 nm was detected using a multifunctional enzyme marker.

RS-CDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method at a temperature of 180 °C for 8 h (Fig. 2a). In order to reveal the structure of as-prepared RS-CDs, the TEM, HRTEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS are measured and presented in Figs. 2 and 3 . RS-CDs were spherical monodisperse nanoparticles with good dispersion in aqueous solution and uniform particle distribution (Fig. 2b). The particle size distribution of RS-CDs was very small, as shown in the histogram, and the average particle size was about 2.75 nm (Fig. 2c). In the high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) image (inset in Fig. 2b), transparent lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm were observed, which were aligned with the (002) plane of graphite (Nagaraj et al., 2022). It was confirmed by XRD analysis that there was an obvious 2θ diffraction peak at about 23.5° in the XRD pattern, which was consistent with the interlayer spacing of the graphite structure and can be attributed to the (002) plane of the graphite carbon (Fig. 2d) (Thongsai et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020).

To determine the surface functional groups of RS-CDs, FT-IR spectra were measured in the range of 500–4000 cm−1. As presented in Fig. 2e, a broad band was observed at 3200-3500 cm−1, which can be attributed to the tensile vibration of N-H/O-H, indicating that RS-CDs had a strong polarity(Huang et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2020). Additionally, the characteristic peaks observed at 1352, 1595, and 1664 cm−1 probably associate with the stretching frequencies about the C-O, C=C, and C=O functional groups (Gao et al., 2020; Thongsai et al., 2019). The stretching vibrational frequencies of C-H can be resulted from the absorption bands at 768, 1195, and 2815 cm−1 (Rajendran et al., 2021). As mentioned above, Rosa sterilis is rich in functional factors such as polyphenols, organic acids, and vitamin c. Therefore, during high-temperature carbonization, the groups enriched in these active ingredients will polymerize on the surface of the carbon dots, resulting in the formation of abundant active groups on RS-CDs. The FT-IR spectra confirmed the chemical composition of the RS-CDs, showing the existence of numerous oxygen-rich functional groups, including phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, on the RS-CDs' framework. These groups had the ability to effectively bond with metal ions, leading to the formation of chelates.

The elemental compositions of the RS-CDs were explored with XPS. Our results of XPS suggested three characteristic peaks, including C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV), and O1s (532 eV), with the corresponding elemental percentages of 71.81%, 1.32%, and 26.87%, respectively (Fig. 3a). Among them, Fig. 3b presents the high-resolution XPS pattern of C1s. It had three peaks at 284.7, 286.5, and 288.0 eV, which could be resulted from C−H/C−C, C−O−H, and C=O (Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022b). The O1s XPS spectrum was displayed in Fig. 3c, with two signals appearing at 531.0 and 532.2 eV, which can be caused by C=O and C-O, respectively (Hui et al., 2021; Niu et al., 2015). The XPS spectra of N1s also indicated dual peak signals at 399.7 and 402.0 eV, indicating that the nitrogen atoms within the RS-CDs were linked to the C atoms within the RS-CDs as both pyrrolic nitrogen (Pyrrolic N) (399.4 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (Graphitic N) (400.9 eV) (Ma et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2017). The XPS results showed the presence of oxygen-rich functional groups on the skeleton of RS-CDs and further verified the FT-IR results. The above results demonstrated that Rosa sterilis successfully synthesized carbon nanodots. After one-step hydrothermal treatment, RS-CDs had a graphite-like structure and corresponding characteristic groups, and had certain stability and hydrophilicity.

The optical properties of RS-CDs were analyzed using photoluminescence and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the RS-CDs in an aqueous medium were presented in Fig. 4 a. Two shoulder peaks were found in the absorption spectra at 270–300 nm and 350–375 nm. The absorption peak at 270–300 nm may be caused by the π-π ∗ transition of C = C bond, while the shoulder peak at 350–375 nm may be caused by the n-π ∗ transition of C-O and C-N bonds(Kundu et al., 2022; Munusamy et al., 2023). Additionally, the emission spectrum of RS-CDs was detected to have a maximum emission at 453 nm after 355 nm excitation. According to the inset, the aqueous solution of RS-CDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence visible to the naked eye following irradiation with 365 nm UV light (Fig. 4a, inset on the right). Based on Fig. 4b, the blue fluorescence of the RS-CDs was further demonstrated by the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) coordinates (0.1841, 0.2114) under the excitation of 355 nm UV light, which may be resulted from radiative complexation between holes and electrons, photo-induced charge separation, as well as trapping of the surface sites (Ding et al., 2016; Strauss et al., 2014). In addition, the RS-CDs solution exhibited clear fluorescence-dependent behavior (Fig. 4c). With the excitation wavelength being varied from 320 to 460 nm, the emission center red-shifted from 448 to 530 nm, indicating that the PL emission of RS-CDs showed a relationship to the alteration in the PL excitation wavelength, and the latter change might be related to surface edge defects, quantum-limited-domain effects, and aromatic sp2 bonds (Behera et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2018). Furthermore, the impact of varying concentrations of RS-CDs on the intensity of PL was evaluated. The results demonstrated a progressive rise in PL intensity of RS-CDs as the concentration escalated from 50 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL. However, the PL intensity gradually decreased at higher concentrations, which was due to the self-quenching caused by the excessive fluorophore concentration of RS-CDs. This phenomenon widely exists in fluorescent materials, that is, strong fluorescence will be emitted in dilute solutions, but at high concentrations, the luminescence performance will decrease or even disappear completely, which is mainly due to the energy conversion between molecules in the form of non-radiative rather than fluorescence emission (Li et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022c). As a result, 100 μg/mL was chosen as the best emission concentration for creating RS-CDs.

With the purpose of investigating the PL stability of the RS-CDs, the impacts of various factors, such as pH, UV irradiation time, incubation time, and coexistence time were investigated. Fig. S1a showed that RS-CDs had a more stable PL intensity within the pH range of 2.0–7.0. Nevertheless, the PL intensity tended to decrease at higher pH (pH > 7), which can probably be resulted from the chemical interactions of the surface functional groups of RS-CDs through strong intermolecular hydrogen bonding based on alkaline pH conditions (Choudhury et al., 2017). Surprisingly, the pH of baijiu is mostly 5–6, which is weakly acidic, and the stable PL properties of RS-CDs in acidic environments provide ideal conditions for the ion detection of RS-CDs in baijiu. In addition, to explore the photobleaching resistance of the RS-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of PL was checked every 30 min during UV irradiation for up to 5 h. The findings demonstrated that there was no obvious attenuation in the PL intensity of the RS-CDs (Fig. S1b), indicating that the RS-CDs showed strong resistance to photobleaching. Additionally, with the purpose of investigating the PL stability of RS-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ in baijiu, we explored the impacts of different reaction and coexistence times on the PL intensity of RS-CDs in the detection system. Based on the results, the PL intensity of the RS-CDs first lowered slightly and then stabilized at the initial level during the reaction time from 0 to 10 min. Therefore, we selected 10 min as the optimal reaction time in order to ensure complete reaction of the RS-CDs with Fe3+ (Fig. S1c). In addition, in the case of RS-CDs coexisting with Fe3+ for 1 h, the PL intensity of RS-CDs showed no notable reduction (Fig. S1d). These features clearly indicate that the prepared RS-CDs have stable PL properties in acidic baijiu systems and are suitable for metal ion detection.

The above experimental analysis showed that RS-CDs possess excellent fluorescence properties and structures, and their rich functional groups made them suitable for ion detection. Many studies have shown that CDs are good materials for metal ion detection in aqueous environments. Therefore, the ability of RS-CDs to was evaluated to identify metal ions in baijiu. Therefore, with the aim of investigating the selectivity of RS-CDs for metal ions in baijiu system, we used ethanol-water solution with 53% volume fraction of ethanol to prepare different heavy metal solutions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2 +, Al3+, Pb2 +, Mn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Co+, Ag+) and evaluated the influence of different metal ions on the PL intensities of the RS-CDs under the excitation of 355 nm UV light. Fig. 5 a presents the F/F0 values for different ions, where F refers to the PL intensity of the samples with the added ions (final concentration of 800 μM for each ion), and F0 denotes the PL intensity of RS-CDs without any metal ions. According to the results, RS-CDs responded most significantly to Fe3+, and the fluorescence intensity reduced dramatically after adding Fe3+. Therefore, it was determined that Fe3+ reduced the PL intensity of RS-CDs by 82%. As presented in the literature, Cu2+ and Zn2+interfere with the response of CDs to Fe3+. However, in this study, Cr3+, Cu2+, Pb2 +, and Ag+ exhibited only a slight quenching effect on the RS-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity was almost unchanged upon the present of other metal ions. This phenomenon may be caused from the differences in the surface functionality of CDs owing to the differences in the synthetic raw materials and synthesis methods of different CDs, which leads to the different chemical chelating effects of different CDs on heavy metals. The fluorescence quenching of RS-CDs which were mixed with 13 different metal ions were subsequently measured to confirm the preference of RS-CDs for Fe3+. Based on Fig. 5c, Fe3+ was able to significantly quench the fluorescence of RS-CDs even when co-incubated with various metal ions at the same concentration. Interestingly, the fluorescence quenching of RS-CDs by Fe3+ remained largely unaffected by other metal ions, suggesting a high selectivity of RS-CDs towards Fe3+.These results indicated that the prepared RS-CDs could effectively detect Fe3+ in the simulated baijiu system.

Subsequently, to further investigate the quenching effect of Fe3+, we added Fe3+ solution with a final concentration of 0–1 mM to the simulated baijiu system containing RS-CDs (100 μg/mL) and measured the concentration dependence of its PL. By analyzing the spectra, it was found that the PL intensity of the RS-CDs solution at 453 nm tended to gradually lower with the elevating Fe3+ concentration, and its emission was independent, with no obvious emission shift in the PL spectra (Fig. 5b). In addition, the blue fluorescence was visually significantly weakened (Fig. 5b inset). The association between the quenching efficiency ΔF/F0 and Fe3+ concentration was presented in Fig. 5d. There existed a good linear correlation between ΔF/F0 and the concentration of Fe3+ from 0 to 140 μM (R2 = 0.9978), which can be fitted by an equation of y = 0.00208x + 0.0204, suggesting that the RS-CDs can be analyzed as a quantitative tool for Fe3+ (Fig. 5d inset). In addition, according to the 3 δ/s rule (in which δ refers to the standard deviation of 11 blank tests; s indicates the slope of the standard curve), LOD of RS-CDs was found to be 0.959 μM, which was excellent for some representative biomass-derived CDs (Table S2). Therefore, RS-CDs are expected to be effective tools for Fe3+ detection in baijiu systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Fe3+ detection by RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the literature, the quenching reactions between quenching agents and CDs can typically be classified into internal filtering effect (IFE), transfer of energy resonance energy (FRET), transfer of electrons induced by light (PET), dynamic quenching and static quenching. To explain the quenching mechanism of Fe3+, we recorded the fluorescence lifetimes with and without Fe3+ by transient fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 6 a). Through supplementing 100 μM of Fe3+ to the RS-CDs, it was found that the decay curves of the RS-CDs and the mixture almost overlapped. After fitting the third exponential equation, the fluorescence lifetimes of 0.01 and 0.1 mg/mL RS-CDs were determined to be 2.68 and 2.57 ns, respectively. However, when Fe3+ was supplemented to the RS-CDs at a concentration of 100 μM, insignificant changes in fluorescence lifetimes were observed, with the corresponding values of 2.83 and 2.62 ns, indicating that the presence of Fe3+ would not have a significant effect on the fluorescence stability of RS-CDs, and thus the mechanism of Fe3+ quenching could be due to IFE and static quenching. In order to further demonstrate the finding, we analyzed the UV absorption spectra of the RS-CDs, Fe3+ and RS-CDs/Fe3+ mixture (Fig. 6b). The results showed that absorption peaks at 280 nm were observed for RS-CDs and the mixture, but the UV absorption of the mixture was obviously higher relative to the spectrum of RS-CDs. These findings confirmed the formation of basal complexes between RS-CDs and Fe3+, and that PL quenching was most likely due to the generation of nonfluorescent complexes between Fe3+ and functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups) on the surface of RS-CDs. Before that, a number of studies revealed similar results, suggesting that static quenching is the quenching mechanism of RS-CDs/Fe3+ (Fig. 6c) (Ma et al., 2023; Rajendran et al., 2021; Ren et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of RS-CDs for Fe3+ sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Standard addition experiments were carried out to demonstrate the sensitivity and detection performance of RS-CDs in actual baijiu samples. With the purpose of confirming the applicability of RS-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ in baijiu, baijiu with different aroma types and produced in different production regions was purchased from supermarkets (Fig. S2), and the PL intensities were recorded by fixing the final concentration of RS-CDs to 100 μg/mL, adding different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions to different brands of baijiu with different aroma types and incubating them for 10 min based on the condition of room temperature. With the purpose of calculating the additive recovery, the concentration of Fe3+ was calculated and compared with the actual concentration based on the standard curve. The Fe3+ content in the original baijiu samples was zero, as determined by ICP-OES.

It was found that the spiked recoveries of RS-CDs for the detection of Fe3+ in different brands of sauce, strong, light, and rice-flavor baijiu were all in the range of 84–108.81% (Fig. 7 ), and the relative standard deviation (RSDs) were all within the range of 0.89–5.34%, and these baijiu were distributed in various provinces of China. This proved that RS-CDs can be adopted for the highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in the four main flavor types of Chinese baijiu. The toxicity of different concentrations of RS-CDs (0–0.3 mg/mL) on SO146698 cells was detected by CCK-8 kit. Fig. S3 showed that RS-CDs had good biocompatibility at the administered concentration and the cells were still able to maintain more than 80% viability at a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL. Therefore, their low toxicity is expected to be used in food applications. The above results indicated that the sensing ability of RS-CDs as well as their low toxicity can realize their quantitative detection of Fe3+ in baijiu system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stress detection of Fe3+ in baijiu samples stored in different containers by RS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the application of RS-CDs in detecting baijiu production and brewing processes, we stored the baijiu in different containers (old enamel cylinder, stainless steel tank, ceramic altar), detected Fe3+ in the baijiu samples in the different containers on days 0, 10, 20, and 30, and compared them using the standard instrument, ICP-OES (Fig. 8 ). The results in Fig. 8 showed that the RS-CDs detected Fe3+ only in the old enamel cylinder, and not in any other containers. The RS-CDs detected 24.38, 54.49 and 79.27 mg/L of Fe3+ in the old enamel cylinder at 0, 10, 20, and 30 d, respectively, whereas the ICP-OES results were 24.40, 54.32 and 78.36 mg/L. The differences between the RS-CDs and ICP-OES results for Fe3+ were not significant. In the short-term 30-day storage of baijiu, no Fe3+ was analyzed in either ceramic or stainless steel tanks. However, Fe3+ in old enamel tanks increased with an increase in storage time, which indicated that RS-CDs can be effectively applied as a quantitative tool for detecting Fe3+ in baijiu, and baijiu stored in old enamel tanks requiredgreater attention to explore the impact of Fe3+ on the flavor of baijiu.

In summary, RS-CDs were successfully synthesized from Rosa sterilis by an in situ hydrothermal method. RS-CDs emitted blue light at 453 nm under 355 nm excitation. Meanwhile, due to the abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface, RS-CDs showed high selectivity and anti-interference to Fe3+ and exhibited a static quenching mechanism. In the simulated baijiu system, the linear relationship between RS-CDs and Fe3+ (0–140 μM) was obvious, with a LOD of 0.959 μM. In addition, RS-CDs were suitable for the detection of Fe3+ in the four kinds of flavor baijiu with the spiked recoveries in the range of 84–108.81%. Meanwhile, the detection efficiency of RS-CDs for Fe3+ in baijiu stored in different containers for a short period of time was not different from that of ICP-OES, but attention should be paid to the effect of Fe3+ on the quality of baijiu with the extension of the storage time in the old enamel cylinder. The above results indicated that, compared with the traditional detection methods, RS-CDs originated from biomass not only have lower biotoxicity, but also have the advantages of convenience, rapidity and accuracy for Fe3+ detection. Compared with previous studies, this study is the first time that Rosa sterilis was prepared as carbon dots and applied to the detection of metal ions in baijiu system. Due to the excellent surface properties and optical properties of RS-CDs, it is expected that they are expected to be combined with other carbon nanomaterials in the future for sensing applications of other heavy metal ions or pollutants in baijiu.

Hongqin Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Guohuan Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yuanlin Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Data curation. Yongguang Huang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors assert that they do not have any conflicting financial interests or personal relationships that may have seemed to impact the research reported in this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support of the current project was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32060534 and 32360571), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK [2023] 118, 166), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Support Project (Grant No. [2023] 343), Guizhou Provincial Department of Information Industry Project (Grant No. [2020]198), Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Guizhou Province (Qianke Zhongyindi [2019]4019), General horizontal project (Grant No. K22-0115-014, Qianrenlingfa [2020]8), Guizhou University Natural Science Special Scientific Research Fund Project (X2021327), and Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (Grant No. ZK[2024] Major Project 016).
